text:
MULTIDISCIPLINARY ONCOLOGY CLINIC NOTE
Pancreatic Tumor Board / Medical Oncology Follow-Up

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Patient presents for multidisciplinary tumor board review and medical oncology consultation following histologic confirmation of solitary hepatic metastasis from recurrent pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, 17 months after curative-intent pancreaticoduodenectomy.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Margaret Osei-Bonsu is a 67-year-old woman of Ghanaian descent with a history of borderline resectable pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) of the pancreatic head and uncinate process, initially diagnosed approximately 18 months ago, who now presents with radiographically and pathologically confirmed recurrence in the form of a solitary hepatic metastasis.

By way of background, her initial presentation was notable for progressive painless obstructive jaundice, an 14-pound weight loss, and epigastric pain radiating to the back. Workup revealed a 3.8 cm hypoenhancing pancreatic head mass abutting the SMA (approximately 180 degrees) with SMV involvement and upstream biliary and pancreatic ductal dilation. CA 19-9 at diagnosis was 1,204 U/mL. EUS-guided biopsy confirmed moderately differentiated pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (CK7+, CK19+, MUC1+, Ki-67 ~35%). She was staged as clinical T3 N1 M0, borderline resectable.

She received a biliary stent (FCSEMS) via ERCP, after which her bilirubin normalized from 8.2 to 1.8 mg/dL, and she then initiated modified FOLFIRINOX (oxaliplatin 85 mg/m2, irinotecan 150 mg/m2, leucovorin 400 mg/m2, 5-FU 2400 mg/m2 over 46 hours, q14 days) as neoadjuvant chemotherapy. She received 6 cycles of FOLFIRINOX before oxaliplatin was discontinued due to grade 3 peripheral sensory neuropathy (interfering with ADLs); she then completed 2 additional cycles with FOLFIRI (fluorouracil, leucovorin, irinotecan) for a total of 8 neoadjuvant cycles. Over this period her CA 19-9 declined from 1,204 to 85 U/mL, and restaging CT showed the primary tumor had decreased to 2.6 cm with SMA abutment reduced to less than 90 degrees and restoration of SMV patency, consistent with a partial treatment response.

Following the multidisciplinary tumor board's recommendation, she underwent consolidation chemoradiation (54 Gy in 28 fractions by IMRT with simultaneous integrated boost; concurrent capecitabine 825 mg/m2 BID on radiation days). Pre-operative CT and PET-CT showed further reduction of the primary mass to 2.3 cm, SMA contact less than 60 degrees, no FDG-avid distant disease (SUVmax 4.1), and CA 19-9 of 52 U/mL.

She subsequently underwent open pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure) with en bloc segmental SMV resection and end-to-end venous reconstruction. Final surgical pathology demonstrated moderately differentiated PDAC, 2.1 cm, ypT3 N1 M0 (1/22 positive lymph nodes), with perineural invasion present, lymphovascular invasion absent, CAP tumor regression grade 2 (moderate response), and an R0 resection with the tightest margin being the SMA/retroperitoneal margin at 1.2 mm. Comprehensive NGS on the resection specimen revealed KRAS p.G12V (VAF 42%), TP53 p.R175H (VAF 38%), homozygous SMAD4 deletion, and CDKN2A/p16 deletion — a high-risk genomic profile — with MSS, low TMB (3 mut/Mb), no BRCA1/2, ATM, or PALB2 pathogenic mutations, and no fusions detected.

The postoperative course was complicated by an ISGPF grade B pancreatic fistula managed conservatively with prolonged drain maintenance, TPN, and octreotide, with eventual drain removal at 6 weeks. She was then initiated on adjuvant gemcitabine/capecitabine (GemCap) per ESPAC-4 data, with gemcitabine 1000 mg/m2 days 1, 8, 15 of a 28-day cycle and capecitabine started at 80% dose (1330 mg/m2/day, days 1-21). Adjuvant therapy was complicated by a minor recurrent pancreatic fistula (grade A, managed conservatively), grade 2 hand-foot syndrome necessitating capecitabine dose reduction to 75%, grade 2 cytopenias requiring gemcitabine dose reduction to 750 mg/m2 and day-15 omissions in multiple cycles, and progressive peripheral neuropathy to grade 2 on background of oxaliplatin-related neuropathy. She completed all 6 planned cycles of adjuvant GemCap at reduced doses. CA 19-9 at end of adjuvant treatment was 19 U/mL.

Post-treatment surveillance CT approximately 8 months after surgery showed no evidence of recurrent or metastatic disease. At routine surveillance approximately 14 months after the Whipple procedure, the patient was doing well — CA 19-9 24 U/mL, CEA 2.2 ng/mL — and had resumed volunteer work at the local library.

At approximately 17 months post-Whipple, routine surveillance CT abdomen/pelvis identified a new 1.4 cm hypodense lesion in hepatic segment VI. CA 19-9 had risen to 89 U/mL (from a nadir of 18 U/mL) and CEA had risen to 4.8 ng/mL. Urgent MRI liver with gadoxetate disodium (Eovist) was performed and showed the segment VI lesion to have hepatobiliary phase hypointensity, restricted diffusion, and arterial rim enhancement with washout — features diagnostic of metastatic disease. No additional hepatic lesions were identified and concurrent CT chest showed no extrahepatic disease.

CT-guided percutaneous core needle biopsy of the hepatic lesion confirmed metastatic adenocarcinoma of pancreatobiliary origin (CK7+, CK19+, MUC1+, CA 19-9+, CK20-, CDX2-, TTF-1-). Repeat NGS on the metastatic sample confirmed an identical driver mutation profile to the primary: KRAS p.G12V, TP53 p.R175H, SMAD4 loss. No new actionable alterations were identified. Microsatellite stable confirmed. BRCA1/2 somatic mutations absent.

The case was reviewed today at the multidisciplinary pancreatic tumor board, attended by hepatobiliary surgery, radiation oncology, interventional radiology, medical oncology, and palliative care.

From the patient's perspective, she reports feeling well at baseline since completing adjuvant therapy. Energy has been good. She has maintained her weight and is eating adequately. She continues pancrelipase (Creon) with meals for exocrine pancreatic insufficiency and notes that her stool consistency and absorption have been acceptable. Her type 2 diabetes (worsened post-pancreatectomy) is managed with metformin and basal insulin glargine; she reports adequate glucose control under endocrinology co-management. She continues to have grade 1 peripheral neuropathy in her fingertips, which she describes as mild numbness, stable and not progressing since completing adjuvant GemCap. She denies new abdominal pain, jaundice, weight loss, or constitutional symptoms. She is appropriately anxious about the new diagnosis but expressed a clear desire to understand her options and to continue fighting. She asks specifically about whether the liver lesion can be removed.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Positive for: mild bilateral fingertip numbness (stable, grade 1 neuropathy), mild fatigue with exertion, mild anxiety related to recurrence diagnosis.
Negative for: fever, chills, night sweats, significant unintentional weight loss, jaundice, scleral icterus, pruritus, abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, significant change in bowel habits, melena, hematochezia, dyspnea, cough, chest pain, lower extremity edema, new neurologic symptoms.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY (carried forward)
1. Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, head and uncinate process — as above
2. Type 2 diabetes mellitus — diet-controlled at initial presentation, now requiring metformin + insulin glargine post-pancreatectomy
3. Hypertension — managed with antihypertensives (see medications)
4. Exocrine pancreatic insufficiency — post-pancreatectomy, on PERT
5. History of biliary obstruction — treated with FCSEMS (ERCP), stent in situ
6. Oxaliplatin-induced peripheral sensory neuropathy — grade 1, stable
7. Post-surgical — status post pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy with SMV resection/reconstruction; history of grade B postoperative pancreatic fistula (resolved)

SOCIAL HISTORY (carried forward)
Retired librarian. Currently volunteers at the local library. Of Ghanaian descent. Former smoker: 15 pack-year history, quit approximately 10 years prior. Denies current alcohol or illicit drug use. Lives independently. Has strong family support. Has a good understanding of her diagnosis and prognosis.

FAMILY HISTORY (carried forward)
No family history of pancreatic cancer. Germline BRCA1/2 testing was performed and returned negative. No other hereditary cancer syndromes identified in family history.

ALLERGIES
No known drug allergies.

MEDICATIONS
- Metformin 1000 mg PO BID
- Insulin glargine (Lantus) 14 units subcutaneously at bedtime (dose per endocrinology)
- Lisinopril 10 mg PO daily (hypertension)
- Amlodipine 5 mg PO daily (hypertension)
- Pancrelipase (Creon 24,000) 3 capsules PO with meals, 1-2 capsules with snacks
- Ondansetron 8 mg PO q8h PRN nausea
- Aspirin 81 mg PO daily
- Multivitamin PO daily
- Vitamin D3 2000 IU PO daily

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital signs: Blood pressure 128/76 mmHg, heart rate 78 bpm (regular), respiratory rate 16/min, temperature 98.4°F (36.9°C), oxygen saturation 97% on room air, weight 138 lbs (62.6 kg), BMI 23.1 kg/m2.

General: Alert, oriented, well-appearing woman in no acute distress. Appears her stated age. Affect is calm but understandably anxious. Well-nourished.

HEENT: Sclerae anicteric. Oropharynx clear without mucositis. No lymphadenopathy in the cervical or supraclavicular regions.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1 and S2. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema.

Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezing, rales, or rhonchi.

Abdomen: Soft, non-distended, non-tender to palpation throughout. Well-healed midline surgical scar from prior Whipple procedure. No palpable hepatomegaly (liver edge not appreciable below the right costal margin). No palpable splenomegaly. No palpable mass. No ascites by exam (no shifting dullness, no fluid wave).

Extremities: No lower extremity edema. Peripheral pulses intact.

Neurological: Alert and oriented x3. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Gait normal. Upper extremity coordination intact. Sensation: mild decreased sensation to light touch in fingertips bilaterally (consistent with known grade 1 oxaliplatin-induced peripheral neuropathy). Lower extremity sensation normal. No new neurologic findings.

Skin: No jaundice. No palmar-plantar erythema or desquamation (HFS resolved since completing GemCap). No rashes.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

Complete blood count: WBC 5.8 x10^3/mcL, hemoglobin 11.2 g/dL (mild anemia, likely nutritional/chronic disease), platelets 198 x10^3/mcL.
Comprehensive metabolic panel: Sodium 138 mEq/L, potassium 4.1 mEq/L, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, BUN 14 mg/dL, glucose 118 mg/dL, total bilirubin 0.6 mg/dL, AST 28 U/L, ALT 32 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 74 U/L, albumin 3.6 g/dL, total protein 6.9 g/dL.
Coagulation: PT/INR 1.0 (within normal limits).
Tumor markers: CA 19-9 89 U/mL (normal <37 U/mL; up from nadir of 18 U/mL); CEA 4.8 ng/mL (normal <3.0 ng/mL; up from nadir of 2.0 ng/mL).
HbA1c: 7.1% (per most recent endocrinology visit).

---

IMAGING RESULTS

Surveillance CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast (approximately 17 months post-Whipple): New 1.4 cm hypodense lesion in hepatic segment VI. No additional hepatic lesions. No peritoneal implants. No pathologic adenopathy. Post-Whipple surgical bed without suspicious soft tissue recurrence.

MRI liver with gadoxetate disodium (Eovist): The segment VI lesion demonstrates hypointensity on hepatobiliary phase, restricted diffusion on DWI (low ADC), and arterial rim enhancement with washout on portal venous phase — appearance diagnostic of metastatic disease. No additional intrahepatic lesions. Biliary stent remains in appropriate position; no biliary dilation.

CT chest: No pulmonary nodules or mediastinal adenopathy. No evidence of thoracic metastatic disease.

PET-CT: Not repeated at this evaluation; the most recent PET-CT (pre-Whipple) had shown no FDG-avid distant disease.

Pathology of liver biopsy (segment VI): Metastatic adenocarcinoma of pancreatobiliary origin. IHC: CK7+, CK19+, MUC1+, CA 19-9+, CK20-, CDX2-, TTF-1-. NGS: KRAS p.G12V, TP53 p.R175H, SMAD4 loss — concordant with primary tumor.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 67-year-old woman with KRAS G12V-mutant, TP53-mutant, SMAD4-deleted, MSS pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, originally borderline resectable, who was treated with a multimodal curative-intent approach comprising biliary decompression, neoadjuvant modified FOLFIRINOX (8 cycles with oxaliplatin discontinued after cycle 6 for grade 3 neuropathy, completed as FOLFIRI), consolidation chemoradiation (54 Gy IMRT with concurrent capecitabine), and ultimately R0 pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy with SMV resection. She completed adjuvant GemCap (6 cycles at reduced doses due to hematologic toxicity, hand-foot syndrome, and neuropathy). She now presents with pathologically confirmed solitary hepatic metastasis (segment VI, 1.4 cm) identified at 17 months post-resection, representing disease recurrence in the metastatic setting.

This case was presented in full at today's multidisciplinary pancreatic tumor board. The key questions deliberated were: (1) Can this oligometastatic disease be addressed with locoregional therapy — surgical hepatic resection, ablation, or stereotactic body radiation — either alone or in combination with systemic therapy? (2) What is the optimal systemic regimen given her prior treatment exposure? (3) What is the appropriate framing of goals of care?

Regarding locoregional therapy: Hepatobiliary surgery was consulted. While technically a solitary 1.4 cm hepatic metastasis in segment VI could be anatomically resectable, the board noted that in the context of pancreatic cancer with its high propensity for micrometastatic dissemination, the clinical benefit of upfront resection in this setting is not established, and there is significant concern that proceeding directly to hepatectomy without systemic therapy would expose the patient to surgical risk without controlling presumed occult microscopic disease. The prior history of abdominal surgery (Whipple with SMV reconstruction) increases the technical complexity and perioperative risk of any hepatic resection. Interventional radiology noted that image-guided thermal ablation (microwave or radiofrequency ablation) could be considered for a lesion of this size and location, though again the board consensus was that systemic therapy should come first. Radiation oncology noted that hepatic SBRT to the segment VI lesion is feasible and could be revisited in the oligometastatic consolidation setting after documentation of systemic disease control. The board decision was unanimous: initiate systemic chemotherapy first, reassess after 2-3 cycles for evidence of systemic disease control and oligometastatic stability, and then discuss locoregional therapy for hepatic consolidation if there is a good response without new metastatic sites.

Regarding systemic therapy: The patient has received, in order: modified FOLFIRINOX (6 cycles) → FOLFIRI (2 cycles, after oxaliplatin discontinuation), then chemoradiation with capecitabine, then adjuvant gemcitabine plus capecitabine (6 cycles). In the metastatic setting, the two best-established first-line regimens for PDAC — FOLFIRINOX/mFOLFIRINOX and gemcitabine/nab-paclitaxel — must be considered against her prior exposure. Her exposure to fluorouracil, leucovorin, and irinotecan in the neoadjuvant setting argues against re-challenging with FOLFIRINOX as optimal first-line palliative therapy, particularly given persistent peripheral neuropathy that would worsen with oxaliplatin rechallenge. Her adjuvant GemCap already included gemcitabine and capecitabine. There is reasonable evidence, however, that gemcitabine/nab-paclitaxel (Abraxane) remains an active and appropriate regimen even in patients who have received prior gemcitabine-based therapy in the adjuvant setting, particularly given the disease-free interval of 17 months and the established systemic activity of nab-paclitaxel in PDAC. The nab-paclitaxel component provides meaningful additional activity distinct from capecitabine, and the disease-free interval of more than one year makes rechallenge with gemcitabine-containing therapy biologically reasonable.

The NAPOLI-3 trial data (NALIRIFOX: nanoliposomal irinotecan, oxaliplatin, 5-FU/leucovorin) in the first-line metastatic setting was discussed; however, given her prior extensive exposure to both oxaliplatin and irinotecan, and grade 3 oxaliplatin-induced neuropathy in the prior setting, this regimen is not felt to be appropriate.

No actionable genomic alterations were identified on NGS of either the primary or the metastatic biopsy: BRCA1/2 wild-type (confirmed on both somatic and germline testing), ATM and PALB2 wild-type, MSS with low TMB (3 mut/Mb) — precluding benefit from olaparib maintenance, atezolizumab, or pembrolizumab immune checkpoint inhibition. KRAS G12V is not currently targetable with approved agents (KRAS G12C inhibitors such as sotorasib and adagrasib do not cover the G12V variant). HER2 amplification absent. The KRAS G12V mutation does, however, remain a potentially relevant target for emerging investigational agents, and clinical trial enrollment should be explored.

Plan:

1. Initiate gemcitabine/nab-paclitaxel for metastatic PDAC. Gemcitabine 1000 mg/m2 IV and nab-paclitaxel (Abraxane) 125 mg/m2 IV on days 1, 8, and 15 of each 28-day cycle. Treatment intent is palliative/disease control, with the primary endpoint being systemic disease control allowing consideration of hepatic locoregional consolidation.

2. Monitor CBC, CMP, and CA 19-9 / CEA at the start of each cycle. Given prior grade 2 cytopenias on adjuvant GemCap, will monitor closely for myelosuppression; G-CSF (filgrastim) will be available as rescue but not initiated prophylactically at this time given current blood counts.

3. Monitor peripheral neuropathy closely given cumulative exposure to oxaliplatin (prior grade 3) and now introduction of nab-paclitaxel, which carries its own significant risk of peripheral neuropathy. Patient counseled that she may experience worsening numbness and/or tingling in hands and feet with nab-paclitaxel; nab-paclitaxel dose reduction or discontinuation will be considered if neuropathy worsens to grade 2 or above.

4. Restaging CT chest, abdomen, and pelvis after 2 cycles (approximately 8 weeks) to assess systemic disease response and determine eligibility for hepatic locoregional consolidation.

5. Clinical trial: Patient will be referred to the oncology clinical trials team for evaluation of eligibility for trials targeting KRAS G12V or other relevant targets in PDAC. Additionally, any available trials for second-line or subsequent therapy in MSS, BRCA-wild-type PDAC will be noted for future reference.

6. Nutrition: Continue pancrelipase (Creon) with meals. Nutrition team consultation to be arranged given ongoing importance of nutritional support during palliative systemic therapy. Albumin 3.6 g/dL — acceptable for initiation of therapy.

7. Diabetes: Continue current regimen per endocrinology. Alert endocrinology to new chemotherapy initiation; nausea and appetite changes may affect glucose management.

8. Goals of care: A full goals of care conversation was conducted today with the patient. She understands that her cancer has returned in the liver and that the goal of treatment at this stage is disease control rather than cure. She expressed her wish to pursue active treatment and to maintain quality of life. She has a durable power of attorney for healthcare designated. Palliative care team was introduced today and will co-follow during palliative chemotherapy for symptom management and ongoing goals-of-care discussions.

9. Follow-up: Patient will be seen in clinic in 2 weeks prior to cycle 1 day 8 for toxicity assessment and labs. She will be seen at the start of cycle 2 (day 29) for full assessment.

Time spent with patient: approximately 75 minutes, including multidisciplinary tumor board review time. Questions answered thoroughly. Patient verbalized understanding of the diagnosis, treatment plan, and next steps.

---

Dictated by: Attending Physician, Medical Oncology — Pancreatic/GI Oncology Program
Co-signed by: Fellow, Hematology/Oncology
CC: Hepatobiliary Surgery, Radiation Oncology, Gastroenterology, Endocrinology, Palliative Care, Primary Care Physician
